ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 褡裢火烧 (Da Lian Huo Shao). Mapped to 'stuffed pancake food product' as the closest defensible FoodOn class covering pan-fried wheat-dough pancakes with savoury filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Pancakes and similar products) used as the closest defensible category for this pan-fried stuffed wheat pancake. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for traditional Beijing-style stuffed fried pancakes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this traditional Chinese stuffed fried pancake preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of 褡裢火烧 is one piece weighing approximately 100–120 g, equivalent to one elongated pan-fried stuffed pancake as served at a Beijing snack stall.

nutrient_source.title: 褡裢火烧 Fried Wheaten Pancake With Pork Fillings — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 褡裢火烧 Fried Wheaten Pancake With Pork Fillings (cr_id G00010149) with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 褡裢火烧 (Da Lian Huo Shao) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: white wheat flour flatbread / Chinese steamed bread (mantou) analogues, which range GI 68–88 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). A value of 70 is used as a conservative estimate reflecting the pan-fried refined wheat flour wrapper with minimal fibre (0.7 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 49.9) / 100 ≈ 34.9, rounded to 34.8 for a 110 g serving (carbohydrate = 45.38 × 1.10 = 49.9 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low recorded sodium at 5 mg/100g; however, the pork filling typically contains soy sauce which contributes additional sodium not fully captured in the HPB record.
hypertension.guidance: The HPB-recorded sodium of 5 mg/100g is very low and poses minimal hypertension risk from the wheat wrapper alone, but patients should be aware that the pork filling with soy sauce will increase actual sodium intake; request reduced soy sauce preparation and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (45.4 g/100g) from refined wheat flour with very low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g), yielding an estimated GI of 70 and a high glycemic load of approximately 34.8 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid 褡裢火烧 due to its high glycemic load driven by refined wheat flour and minimal fibre; if consumed, restrict to half a piece, pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and request wholemeal flour preparation where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the pork filling contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid; total fat is low at 0.38 g/100g per HPB record but this likely reflects the wheat wrapper component only.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the pan-fried preparation and pork filling contribute AGEs and saturated fat that may adversely affect endothelial function, and the high glycemic load can elevate triglycerides — limit to occasional consumption and pair with fibre-rich vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-frying generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation, and the pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid 褡裢火烧 as the combination of pan-fried preparation, refined wheat flour, and pork filling collectively promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; if consumed, limit strictly to small portions and balance with anti-inflammatory omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 褡裢火烧 (Dā lián huǒ shāo), known in English as Fried Wheaten Pancake with Pork Filling, is a traditional Beijing snack originating in the Qing Dynasty. The dish consists of an elongated, pan-fried wheat flour pancake stuffed with a savoury pork filling typically seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, spring onion, and sesame oil. The outer crust is crispy and golden from pan-frying, while the interior remains soft and juicy. The shape is said to resemble ancient Chinese money pouches (褡裢), giving the dish its distinctive name. It is made to order and commonly served as a street food or snack in Beijing-style Chinese restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 207 kcal, 4.05 g protein, 0.38 g fat, and 45.38 g carbohydrate with only 0.7 g dietary fibre, reflecting the dominant refined wheat flour wrapper. Sodium is recorded at 5 mg/100g for the HPB entry, though actual sodium from the full dish including pork filling with soy sauce will be higher. The estimated glycemic index is 70 (high), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 34.8 per 110 g serving, making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Iron content is notable at 2.32 mg/100g. The dish contains wheat (gluten) and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010149) with a recorded confidence of 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. The HPB is a primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible wheat flatbread analogue, documented transparently.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item (G00010149), and no supplementary approved source was available to supply these values for this specific traditional Beijing preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database). This is plausible for the wheat wrapper component but may underrepresent the full dish including pork filling; the HPB value is preserved as recorded. 3. Sodium of 5 mg/100g reflects the wheat flour pancake component as recorded by HPB; actual dish sodium including pork filling with soy sauce will be higher. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat flour flatbread/Chinese steamed bread analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for 褡裢火烧 was found in any approved GI source. 5. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are functional translations provided for Singapore multilingual compliance; these are descriptive translations rather than established local names for this Beijing-origin dish.
